title: Spike: Phase 2 - Can batch UI stay scan-friendly as controls and totals are added?
close_reason: YES: current full-width Batch surface can stay scan-friendly if Phase 2 enforces summary-first layout, compact toolbar/totals, conditional row controls, and responsive row constraints.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - backend packaging boundary yes/no
close_reason: YES: Phase 1 architecture is packaging-compatible if it proves the managed-backend seam now and defers full unzip-and-run packaging UAT to Phase 4.

title: Spike: Phase 2 - Can app-side queue orchestration satisfy D8 over single-job API?
close_reason: YES: app-owned queue orchestration over createDownloadJob/getJob can satisfy D8 for Phase 2 when pause/resume are queue-level only, retry is terminal-row only, and tests use fake clients/timers.

title: Resolve Review P2: Stale async queue work can mutate a rebuilt batch queue
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

The batch queue runner can apply an old asynchronous backend response to a newer queue. It only tracks whether the runner is active, not which queue run the response belongs to.

## What The Code Does Today

- `start()` replaces `rows`, clears sets, and sets `active = true`.
- `submitRow()` awaits `createDownloadJob()` and then checks only `active` before looking up a row by id.
- Parsed queue row ids are reused (`row-1`, `row-2`, etc.) when the user rebuilds a queue.
- Tests cover duplicate scheduling while a submit is pending, but not stop/restart or rebuild while old async work is unresolved.

## Why This Is A Problem

- Queue rebuild is normal batch-management behavior.
- A stale response can attach an old backend job id/status to a different URL in the current queue.
- That corrupts the user-visible row state and aggregate totals.

## Concrete Failure Scenario

- The user starts a queue containing URL A as `row-1`.
- Before `createDownloadJob()` resolves, the user edits the text, rebuilds a new queue, and starts URL B, also as `row-1`.
- The delayed response for URL A returns with `job-a` while the runner is active again.
- The runner finds the new `row-1` and records `job-a` against URL B.

## Evidence

**File:** `src/services/batchQueueRunner.ts`
**Line(s):** 141-170

```ts
const response = await options.backendClient.createDownloadJob({ url: row.normalizedUrl! });
if (!active) {
  return;
}

const currentRow = getRowById(rowId);
if (currentRow === undefined) {
  return;
}

currentRow.currentJobId = response.jobId;
```

**File:** `src/services/batchQueueRunner.ts`
**Line(s):** 236-243

```ts
const start = (initialRows: BatchQueueRow[]): void => {
  clearAllPollTimers();
  submittingRowIds.clear();
  rows = initialRows.map((row) => ({ ...row }));
  active = true;
```

**Why this is a problem:** `active` becoming true for a new queue is not proof that an old async continuation belongs to that queue.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: Add a run generation token
**Pros:** Small, direct, and protects both submit and poll continuations.
**Cons:** Requires touching every async continuation path in the runner.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B - Use globally unique row ids per queue build
**Pros:** Reduces row id collision risk.
**Cons:** Still does not fully protect old continuations after stop/start unless paired with a run token.
**Effort:** Small

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A regression test starts queue A with a deferred submit, starts queue B, resolves queue A, and proves queue B is unchanged.
- [ ] A regression test proves stale poll completions cannot mutate a rebuilt/stopped queue.
- [ ] The runner ignores submit and poll continuations whose captured generation does not match the current generation.
- [ ] Normal scheduling, pause/resume, and retry behavior still pass.
close_reason: Completed: generation-guarded batch queue continuations with stale submit/poll regression coverage

title: Spike: Phase 3 - managed cookie config write path
close_reason: YES: write cookies into managed YAML; cookie file only works when YAML opts into auto cookie loading and backend is restarted.

title: Resolve Review P2 Batch accepts non HTTP schemes
description: Batch parser validates supported host but not protocol. Evidence: src/services/batchQueue.ts checks hostname then normalizes, so ftp://www.douyin.com/video/1 can become a waiting row and be submitted by src/services/batchQueueRunner.ts. Acceptance: reject non-http and non-https schemes, keep valid Douyin https URLs working, and add regression tests proving createDownloadJob is not called for ftp/file scheme rows.
close_reason: Completed: parser rejects non-http schemes and runner regression proves ftp/file rows are never submitted

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Tauri sidecar lifecycle yes/no
close_reason: YES: Phase 1 can implement app-managed lifecycle if it proves dev Python runtime now and defers final packaged sidecar proof to Phase 4.

title: Spike: Phase 2 - Can retry avoid duplicate in-flight submissions and bad totals?
close_reason: YES: retry can be terminal-row-gated with app-side in-flight row state; totals must be recomputed from current row states after retry resets.

title: Resolve Review P2: Skipped-row retry contract is broader than implementation
description:
## Plain-Language Summary

Phase 2 says users can retry failed and skipped queue rows, but the current UI/runtime only proves retry for failed rows. Parser-created skipped rows are never marked retry-eligible, so the visible contract is broader than the implemented behavior.

## What The Code Does Today

- `parseBatchQueueInput()` creates blank, invalid, unsupported, and duplicate rows through `createSkippedRow()`.
- `createSkippedRow()` always sets `retryEligible: false`.
- `isBatchRowRetryEligible()` only allows retry when `retryEligible` is true, status is `failed` or `skipped`, a normalized URL exists, and no current job is attached.
- Current tests intentionally prove parser-skipped duplicates are not retried, while failed backend rows can be retried globally or per row.

## Why This Is A Problem

- Phase 2 exit criteria in `history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-2-contract.md` say retry is available for failed and skipped rows.
- A reviewer or user reading the contract expects skipped rows with recoverable URLs to be retryable, but the app disables retry for all parser-created skipped rows.
- This is a contract mismatch rather than a crash; failed-row retry is implemented, but skipped-row semantics need to be clarified or implemented.

## Concrete Failure Scenario

- A user pastes two equivalent Douyin URLs and one is marked skipped as a duplicate.
- The user wants to retry only the skipped row from the table.
- The row-level Retry button is disabled because `retryEligible` is false.
- The user cannot complete the contract-described skipped-row retry path from the current queue.

## Evidence

**File:** `src/services/batchQueue.ts`
**Line(s):** 111-138

```ts
export function isBatchRowRetryEligible(row: BatchQueueRow): boolean {
  return (
    row.retryEligible &&
    (row.status === failed || row.status === skipped) &&
    row.normalizedUrl !== null &&
    row.currentJobId === null
  );
}

function createSkippedRow(...): BatchQueueRow {
  return {
    ...
    status: skipped,
    retryEligible: false,
```

**File:** `history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-2-contract.md`
**Line(s):** 34-41

```md
Retry is available for failed and skipped rows, resetting selected rows into waiting state and resubmitting them without rebuilding the whole queue.
```

**Why this is a problem:** The contract says skipped-row retry is available, but the row model disables retry for every parser-skipped row.

## Proposed Solutions

### Option A - Recommended: Narrow the contract and UAT wording to failed rows only
**Pros:** Matches current implementation and avoids pretending blank/invalid/parser-skipped rows can become valid without rebuilding input.
**Cons:** Reduces the original Phase 2 promise and should be explicit in docs/UAT.
**Effort:** Small

### Option B - Implement recoverable skipped-row retry
**Pros:** Preserves the broader contract for skipped rows that have a valid `normalizedUrl`, such as selected duplicate handling if the product wants that behavior.
**Cons:** Needs careful semantics so blank, invalid, and unsupported rows stay non-retryable and duplicate retry does not create surprising duplicate downloads.
**Effort:** Medium

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Decide whether Phase 2 retry scope means failed rows only or recoverable skipped rows too.
- [ ] If failed-only, update Phase 2 contract/UAT wording and tests so the app no longer claims skipped-row retry.
- [ ] If skipped retry remains required, implement row-level retry for clearly recoverable skipped rows while blank/invalid/unsupported rows remain disabled.
- [ ] Add or update tests proving the selected contract.
close_reason: Completed: narrow Phase 2 retry contract to failed rows and enforce failed-only row retry eligibility

title: Resolve Review P2 Failed rows hide error reasons
description: Failed batch rows always show generic retry text before row.lastError is checked. Evidence: src/components/QueueTable.tsx getReasonLabel returns Download failed for every failed row, hiding cookie/config details from the visible row. D11 requires actionable user-facing guidance. Acceptance: failed rows show mapped friendly reasons, cookie failures point to cookie refresh/fetch-again, generic failures still mention retry, diagnostics retain technical detail.
close_reason: Completed: failed queue rows now map row.lastError to friendly guidance with cookie refresh and retry hints

title: Spike: Phase 2 - Are pause resume semantics truthful without backend cancellation?
close_reason: YES: Phase 2 can truthfully define pause as blocking future queue starts only while running backend jobs continue to terminal state; no backend cancellation API is needed for this phase.

title: Resolve Review P2 Batch retry is bulk only
description: Batch retry is implemented only as retryEligibleRows bulk retry, with one Retry failed button and no row action. D8 requires retry per job. Failure scenario: user wants to retry one cookie-related failed row but app retries all eligible failures. Acceptance: add row-scoped retry such as retryRow(rowId), wire a row action, keep bulk retry separate if retained, and test row-scoped retry does not resubmit unrelated rows.
close_reason: Completed: added row-scoped retryRow flow with row action while preserving bulk retry

title: Spike: Phase 3 - history records final retry outcomes
close_reason: YES: Phase 3 history can record final current single/batch retry outcomes if workers use app-owned durable upsert history keyed by logical item/run-row ids, write only current terminal states, keep attempt details in Logs, and never treat backend /api/v1/jobs as durable history.

title: Windows Desktop Downloader UI
description:
## Feature Context

Build the first Windows desktop app experience for the existing Douyin downloader.

Locked decisions from CONTEXT.md: D1 desktop app, D15 app-managed backend, D16 portable first distribution, D2 single-link flow, D8 full batch in first version, D9 Single/Batch equal weight, D17 first UI scope limited to core download modes.

## Planning Artifacts

- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/CONTEXT.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/discovery.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/approach.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-plan.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-1-contract.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-1-story-map.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-2-contract.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-2-story-map.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-3-contract.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-3-story-map.md

## Current Planning State

Phase 1 and Phase 2 are closed. Phase 3 is prepared for validation through beads douyin-downloader-app-irx.20 through douyin-downloader-app-irx.27. Do not implement Phase 3 until khuym:validating approves it. Do not implement Phase 4 portable packaging work until its own phase contract and beads exist.

## Success Criteria

- Phase plans and current-phase artifacts remain traceable from feature to phase to story to bead.
- Only the approved current phase is prepared for validation and execution.
- Later phases are not implemented until their own planning/validation gates are complete.

comments.author: unknown
comments.text: Reopened: Reopened for Phase 3 planning artifacts and validation handoff.

title: Phase 1 / Story 1: Scaffold the desktop app shell and test harness
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The user can open a Windows desktop app, wait for a managed backend to become ready, run one single-link download, and open the output folder.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 batch queue can reuse the desktop shell, status layout, and backend client foundations.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - App shell and backend readiness
What Happens: The wrapper repo becomes a real desktop app project with a restrained Windows utility layout and test harness.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that a Tauri Windows desktop app scaffold exists and is not a browser-localhost page.
Unlocks: Backend lifecycle/readiness work can attach to a real shell and tested app state.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Build a Tauri v2 + React + TypeScript desktop app in douyin-downloader-app and keep the UI as a practical, status-forward Windows utility.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- package.json
- vite.config.ts
- tsconfig.json
- src/main.tsx
- src/app/App.tsx
- src/components/* only for initial shell components
- src/styles/app.css
- src/tests/* for scaffold/app-shell tests
- src-tauri/* only enough to create a runnable Tauri shell

## Task

Create the minimal Tauri v2 + React + TypeScript + Vite scaffold needed for the desktop app. The first screen should be the actual downloader workspace shell, not a landing page. Include visible Single and Batch mode placement according to D9, but implement only the Phase 1 Single path behavior later.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `npm run test` or equivalent scaffold tests pass.
- `npm run build` passes.
- `npm run tauri dev` can open a desktop app window in development.
- UI shell visually matches a clean Windows utility, not a media-heavy page or developer dashboard.
- No backend functionality is faked in production code beyond clear test doubles.
notes: MSVC linker blocker resolved. Resume bead to fix remaining scaffold gaps: tauri package metadata and src-tauri/icons/icon.ico so tauri dev can launch.
close_reason: Completed: fixed tauri scaffold blockers (package metadata + icon) and verified tauri dev launch

title: Phase 2 / Story 2: Implement queue runner over backend jobs
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 2 - Batch queue is first-class
What Changes: Valid batch rows can be submitted as backend jobs and tracked deterministically.
Unlocks Next: UI can show active row/job and aggregate counts.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Queue execution and row polling
What Happens: Implement a queue runner service that maps valid waiting rows to createDownloadJob/getJob calls.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for backend job mapping, row polling, and queue counts.
Unlocks: Execution UI wiring and pause/resume/retry controls.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Use app-owned queue orchestration over the existing single-job REST API unless validating proves a backend change is required.
From phase-2-contract.md: queue execution must respect a small app-side concurrency limit and reuse Phase 1 job state mapping.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/services/batchQueueRunner.ts or equivalent new service module
- src/services/backendClient.ts only if type additions are needed
- src/tests/batch-queue-runner.test.ts or equivalent
- no backend files unless validating explicitly changes the contract

## Task

Implement deterministic queue execution with fakeable timers/client. The runner should submit valid waiting rows, store job ids, poll row jobs to terminal state, update row counts/errors, respect concurrency, and never submit skipped/invalid rows.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover start, concurrency limit, row job id assignment, running/success/failed transitions, aggregate totals, and backend submit/poll failures.
- Tests do not sleep or call live Douyin/backend services.
- Runner has explicit guardrails against duplicate in-flight submissions for the same row.
- Existing single-download tests remain green.
notes:
Validation spike constraints:
- Implement app-owned orchestration over createDownloadJob/getJob; do not add a backend batch API for Phase 2.
- Use default app-side concurrency limit 2. If a constant is introduced, name it clearly and test default limit 2; no user setting is required in Phase 2.
- Use fakeable timers/client tests; no sleeps, live backend, Douyin network, or cookies.
- Guard each row against duplicate in-flight submission before and around async createDownloadJob.
- In bead .10, expose the scheduler state/seam needed to block future submissions, but do not build user-facing pause/resume/retry controls. Bead .12 owns the controls and final pause/resume/retry commands.
- The scheduler gate must never call backend pause/cancel; active submitted jobs continue polling to terminal state.
close_reason: Completed: implemented deterministic queue runner with concurrency guard, row polling, failure handling, and aggregate snapshots.

title: Phase 2 / Story 2: Wire batch execution UI and totals
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 2 - Batch queue is first-class
What Changes: Users can start a visible batch and watch rows/counts update in the app.
Unlocks Next: Pause/resume/retry controls can operate on real UI state.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Queue execution and row polling
What Happens: Wire the queue runner into Batch mode, rendering active URL/job, row statuses, and aggregate totals.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for active-job visibility and success/failed/skipped counts.
Unlocks: Story 3 queue controls.

## Planning Context

From phase-2-contract.md: the UI must show active URL/job, running count, success/failed/skipped totals, and terminal batch summary later.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/app/App.tsx
- src/components/BatchDownloadPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/QueueTable.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/BatchStatusPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx and/or focused Batch UI tests
- src/styles/app.css

## Task

Connect Batch mode controls to the queue runner. Add Start behavior, row state rendering, active URL/job display, and aggregate count display while preserving the existing Single mode behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove clicking Start submits valid waiting rows through a fake backend client.
- UI shows running row state, backend job id when available, and aggregate totals.
- Skipped/invalid rows affect skipped/invalid totals without backend submission.
- Existing Single mode tests continue to pass.
notes:
Validation spike constraints:
- Render active URL/job, backend job id, row states, and aggregate totals from app row state selectors.
- Skipped/invalid rows affect row-derived totals without backend submission.
- Keep execution UI compact: one toolbar/totals region, stable rows, no raw backend diagnostics in the main table.
- Show paused queue state separately from running row state.
close_reason: Completed: wire batch start runner UI with row job/totals and tests

title: Phase 2 / Story 3: Add pause resume and retry controls
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 2 - Batch queue is first-class
What Changes: Users can control the batch queue after it starts.
Unlocks Next: Batch completion can summarize a controlled queue.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Pause, resume, and retry
What Happens: Add truthful pause-future-starts, resume-waiting-work, and retry-eligible-rows behavior.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for pause/resume/retry and duplicate-submit safety.
Unlocks: Story 4 completion summary and UAT proof.

## Planning Context

From phase-2-contract.md: pause does not cancel active backend jobs; running jobs continue until terminal state. Retry only resets failed rows and skipped rows that the row model explicitly marks retry eligible, and must not duplicate in-flight jobs.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/services/batchQueueRunner.ts or equivalent
- src/services/batchQueue.ts or equivalent
- src/components/BatchDownloadPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/QueueTable.tsx or equivalent
- src/tests/batch-queue-runner.test.ts
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx and/or focused Batch UI tests

## Task

Implement pause, resume, and retry controls at service and UI level. The copy/control states must make clear that pause prevents new starts only, active backend jobs keep running, and retry applies only to row-model eligible terminal rows that are not in flight. Parser-skipped blank, unsupported, invalid, or duplicate inputs remain visible but are not retry eligible unless the user changes/reparses them into unique supported URLs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove pause prevents starting additional waiting rows while active rows continue polling.
- Tests prove resume starts remaining waiting rows.
- Tests prove retry resets and resubmits only row-model eligible failed/skipped rows.
- Tests prove parser-skipped blank, unsupported, invalid, or duplicate rows are not retry eligible without user edit/reparse.
- UI controls are disabled/enabled appropriately to prevent duplicate in-flight submissions and to avoid offering retry when no retry-eligible rows exist.
notes:
Validation spike constraints:
- Pause label/copy must mean pause new starts only; active backend jobs keep polling to terminal state.
- Resume restarts scheduling for waiting rows only.
- Retry is terminal-row-gated for eligible failed/skipped rows and must never touch submitting/running rows.
- Retry must lock/reset eligible rows before async submission and final totals must be recomputed from current row states.
- Retry eligibility must come from the row model, not only from the status label: parser-skipped blank/unsupported/duplicate inputs remain visible but are not retry eligible unless changed/reparsed into unique supported URLs.
close_reason: Completed: pause/resume/retry controls wired with truthful runner semantics and UI gating.

title: Phase 2 / Story 4: Add batch completion summary and actions
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 2 - Batch queue is first-class
What Changes: A completed batch ends with clear summary and practical output action.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 can be verified and Phase 3 can attach recovery/history/logs.

## Story Context

Story: Story 4 - Batch completion actions and proof
What Happens: Add terminal batch summary and output-folder action reuse from Phase 1.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for completion summary, row/result context, and practical actions.
Unlocks: Phase 2 UAT proof bead.

## Planning Context

From phase-2-contract.md: final totals must match row states, and output actions should be available after terminal queue state.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/app/App.tsx
- src/components/BatchDownloadPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/BatchStatusPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/QueueTable.tsx or equivalent
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx and/or focused Batch UI tests
- src/styles/app.css

## Task

Close the batch UX loop with terminal summary messaging, final aggregate counts, row context for failures/skips, and open-output-folder action from the batch surface. Keep diagnostics separate from the main friendly status surface.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove terminal batch summary appears only when the queue is complete.
- Final success/failed/skipped totals match row states.
- Batch open-folder action reuses the validated Phase 1 open-folder behavior.
- Main batch surface stays friendly; raw details remain in diagnostics/testable caches where applicable.
notes:
Validation spike constraints:
- Terminal summary must use row-state-derived final totals after retries, not accumulated events or backend job-list history.
- A failed row that later succeeds after retry should count as one success row in the final batch summary.
- Reuse the existing output-folder action; keep friendly row failure/skip context in the main surface and raw diagnostics elsewhere.
close_reason: Completed: terminal batch summary, friendly row context, and batch open-folder action.

title: Phase 2: Verify batch queue flow end to end
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 2 - Batch queue is first-class
What Changes: The phase has verification evidence for start, pause, resume, retry, totals, and output action.
Unlocks Next: Phase 3 planning/validation after Phase 2 execution and review are green.

## Story Context

Story: Story 4 - Batch completion actions and proof
What Happens: Run and document Phase 2 automated verification and UAT evidence.
Contributes To: The phase exit state as a whole.
Unlocks: Handoff to reviewing/next phase after validation and execution, not direct swarming.

## Planning Context

From phase-2-contract.md: UAT must prove paste/import, start, pause, resume, retry failed rows, row-matching totals, and output action. Real Douyin/cookies are not required for automated proof; fake backend behavior is acceptable when explicitly disclosed.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- package scripts only if needed for verification
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-2-uat.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/evidence/* phase2 logs
- no broad feature implementation except small verification fixes from prior beads

## Task

Run and document Phase 2 verification after implementation beads complete. Include app tests, build, and a deterministic UAT/smoke path that exercises Batch paste/import, start, pause, resume, retry, terminal summary, totals, and output action with fake or controlled backend behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Phase 2 automated tests pass.
- npm run build passes.
- UAT evidence documents batch start, pause, resume, retry, final totals, and output action.
- Any unavailable proof is reported as a blocker, not silently treated as green.
notes:
Validation spike constraints:
- UAT must prove app-owned queue behavior without claiming a backend batch API was added.
- Evidence must include paste/import, start, pause-new-starts while active row finishes, resume, retry-after-failure, final row-matching totals, and output action.
- Include a scan check for normal desktop and narrow width: compact toolbar, one totals strip, readable rows, and truthful pause wording.
close_reason: Completed Phase 2 verification/UAT evidence (tests, build, proof matrix).

title: Spike: Phase 2 - Is app-side orchestration over single-job API sufficient for D8 queue semantics?
close_reason: YES: App-owned queue over existing single-job API is sufficient for Phase 2 D8 semantics with explicit row-state contract.

title: Spike: Phase 2 - Can pause/resume stay truthful without active-job cancel support?
close_reason: YES: Pause/resume remains truthful by gating future starts only while active jobs continue to terminal state.

title: Spike: Phase 2 - Can retry failed/skipped rows be implemented without duplicate in-flight submissions?
close_reason: YES: Retry can be limited to failed/skipped non-inflight rows with duplicate-submit guardrails.

title: Spike: Phase 2 - Can batch UI stay scan-friendly while exposing controls and totals?
close_reason: YES: UI density risk is manageable with staged story decomposition and explicit utility-style constraints.

title: Resolve Review P1 Import adapter fails
description: Blocking P1 review bead. Import URLs is user-visible, but src/services/batchImportAdapter.ts always throws while tests mock a successful adapter. D8 requires batch import file behavior. Acceptance: real desktop file-selection/read path works, cancel/error paths are friendly, and tests cannot pass with a hardcoded throwing adapter.
close_reason: Completed: wired real batch import picker/read adapter with friendly cancel/error and enforced by adapter tests

title: Phase 1 / Story 1: Implement backend lifecycle readiness and diagnostics
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The app owns backend startup/readiness instead of requiring the user to run a terminal command.
Unlocks Next: Single URL submission can rely on a ready local backend.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - App shell and backend readiness
What Happens: The app starts or attaches to the managed backend, polls /api/v1/health, shows starting/ready/error state, and captures diagnostics separately.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that backend readiness comes from the app-managed process and health endpoint.
Unlocks: Story 2 can submit one URL through the real backend client.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Use Tauri sidecar/process management and treat sidecar lifecycle as HIGH risk for validating. Do not require users to start python run.py --serve manually in the normal happy path.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src-tauri/src/backend.rs
- src-tauri/src/main.rs
- src/services/backendLifecycle.ts or equivalent frontend bridge
- src/components/backend readiness/status component files
- src/tests/backend lifecycle tests

## Task

Implement the app/backend lifecycle abstraction for Phase 1. It should support a test fake, a development path that can start the existing Python server or attach to a configured local process, health polling against /api/v1/health, timeout/error state, and diagnostic capture for the later Logs panel.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover starting, ready, timeout/error, and stopped/cleanup states using fakes.
- Health readiness is based on /api/v1/health, not process existence alone.
- Main UI sees only user-facing backend readiness state; raw diagnostics are kept separately.
- A dev smoke path proves the app can reach a backend without manually opening a browser UI.
- Validating has enough evidence to decide whether sidecar lifecycle needs a spike before execution.
notes: Executor clarification after validation: default Phase 1 runtime path is spawning the sibling backend in dev-python mode with the managed runtime config. Attach mode is only a configured development fallback when an explicit host/port is provided. Record smoke evidence for dev Python lifecycle; explicitly state packaged sidecar execution is deferred to Phase 4 unless separately exercised. Implement a runtime-neutral backend lifecycle abstraction with at least dev-python now and packaged-sidecar later. Dev mode may spawn sibling backend with python run.py --serve --serve-host 127.0.0.1 --serve-port <port> --config <managed-config> --path <absolute-output-folder>. Readiness must poll GET /api/v1/health, not process existence. Capture stdout/stderr diagnostics separately. Handle spawn failure, early exit, health timeout, port conflict/wrong process, and cleanup. Cleanup must terminate only the app-started process; attached external processes are left running. Tauri shell permissions must be scoped to backend command/sidecar and known args, never generic shell execution. Backend lifecycle must accept a generated runtime config path and absolute output folder before startup, and smoke evidence must record runtime mode, host/port, health response, diagnostics capture, timeout/error behavior, cleanup behavior, and whether packaged sidecar proof was deferred.
close_reason: Completed: backend lifecycle readiness now polls /api/v1/health with separate diagnostics and managed cleanup semantics

title: Phase 3 / Story 1: Persist output folder and managed config settings
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: Settings become durable app state instead of temporary React state.
Unlocks Next: Advanced controls and cookie recovery can update the same managed config boundary.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - Persist settings and scoped controls
What Happens: Integrate durable output-folder/settings persistence with managed config serialization.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for persisted output folder and safe backend config handoff.
Unlocks: Collapsed advanced controls can persist first-version options.

## Planning Context

From phase-3-contract.md: output folder and selected first-version advanced options must survive restart, and submit must be blocked or backend-ready after config changes.
From approach.md: the app owns a managed config file before backend start/job submission.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- Runtime proof and settings tests must stay separate; green store tests do not prove the desktop-managed backend is ready for the changed config.

## File Scope

- src/services/settingsStore.ts
- src/tests/settings-store.test.ts
- src/app/App.tsx only for wiring settings state into app lifecycle
- src-tauri/src/* only if a narrow settings path command is needed

## Task

Make the default output folder durable across app launches and ensure the managed runtime config is written atomically from the persisted setting. Keep the implementation focused on the settings/config boundary; do not build the advanced panel here.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove initial settings load, update, restart/reload, and atomic managed config write.
- Tests prove only absolute Windows-safe paths are accepted for output folder/config path.
- App startup reads persisted output folder before enabling submit.
- Config version/readiness behavior remains explicit: jobs cannot submit against stale backend config.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- Managed config YAML is the runtime authority for path, scoped options, and cookies; do not overwrite cookies/options when serializing path updates.
- Every accepted settings/config write increments a config generation, marks backend readiness stale, restarts the managed backend, and only unblocks submission after health passes for that same generation.
- Single submit, Batch start/resume/retry, and queue auto-scheduling must all use the same current-generation readiness gate.
- Prefer blocking or staging settings edits while jobs are active unless an explicit interrupting restart UI is implemented.
close_reason: Completed: persist output folder and managed config readiness wiring

title: Phase 3 / Story 1: Add collapsed scoped advanced controls
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: Power users can set first-version downloader options without cluttering the main workflow.
Unlocks Next: Cookie recovery can share the same config panel and managed config write path.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - Persist settings and scoped controls
What Happens: Add a collapsed advanced controls panel for in-scope core options only.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for D3/D4/D17 scoped controls and safe config serialization.
Unlocks: Cookie recovery UI can sit beside settings without exposing deferred modes.

## Planning Context

From CONTEXT.md: D3 requires visible power-user options, D4 says advanced options are collapsed by default, and D17 defers live/comments/transcript/discovery/search.
From phase-3-contract.md: deferred modes must remain absent from the first UI.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- User-visible adapters and controls need direct boundary tests, not only mocked UI assertions.

## File Scope

- src/components/AdvancedOptionsPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/services/settingsStore.ts or a narrow advanced settings model module
- src/app/App.tsx
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx
- src/tests/settings-store.test.ts or focused advanced settings tests
- src/styles/app.css

## Task

Add collapsed-by-default advanced controls for only the first-version core downloader options. Persist selected values through the settings/config store and keep deferred capabilities out of the UI.

## Acceptance Criteria

- UI tests prove the advanced panel is collapsed by default and can be expanded.
- Tests prove selected in-scope options persist and serialize into managed config.
- Tests prove deferred modes such as live, comments, transcript, discovery, search, mobile/LAN, and installer options are not exposed.
- Changing settings keeps submit blocked until backend readiness catches up or an explicit restart path completes.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- Advanced options must serialize through the same managed YAML authority as output path and cookies.
- Changing options must mark the backend stale and keep all job submission paths blocked until current-generation health passes.
- Deferred modes remain absent: live, comments, transcript, discovery, search, mobile/LAN, installer, external-backend mode, notifications, and server tuning.

Exact Phase 3 in-scope advanced controls:
1. Asset toggles, boolean defaults from backend config: music=true -> YAML music; cover=true -> cover; avatar=true -> avatar; json=true -> json.
2. Folder organization, boolean default true: folderstyle -> YAML folderstyle.
3. Concurrency, integer range 1-10 default 5: thread -> YAML thread.
4. Retry count, integer range 0-10 default 3: retry_times -> YAML retry_times.
5. Proxy, optional string default empty: proxy -> YAML proxy; accept blank for disabled.
6. Browser fallback for profile pagination, collapsed sub-row defaults: browser_fallback.enabled=true, browser_fallback.headless=false, browser_fallback.max_scrolls=240, browser_fallback.idle_rounds=8, browser_fallback.wait_timeout_seconds=600.
7. User/collection download modes, multi-select default [post]: mode -> YAML mode. Allowed only: post, like, mix, music, collect, collectmix. Exclude allmix from UI; if compatibility is needed, normalize allmix to mix internally rather than displaying it.
8. Per-mode limits for allowed modes, integer >=0 default 0 except preserve current default if existing settings have a value: number.post/like/mix/music/collect/collectmix -> YAML number.*. 0 means unlimited.
9. Incremental toggles for supported modes only: increase.post/like/mix/music -> YAML increase.*. Do not expose increase.collect or increase.collectmix because backend docs say collection modes do not support incremental stop.

Quality note:
- Do not add a quality selector in Phase 3. Backend README says highest-quality selection is automatic from the bitrate ladder; there is no stable config key for quality selection in the current backend contract.

Cookie/config note:
- Cookie fetch/manual import controls belong to beads .22/.23. This bead may show current cookie/config readiness state but must not implement cookie capture.
close_reason: Completed: add collapsed scoped advanced controls with managed settings serialization

title: Phase 3 / Story 2: Implement cookie recovery service boundary
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: The app has a real boundary for refreshing or importing Douyin cookies.
Unlocks Next: Single and Batch cookie error UI can trigger recovery actions.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Cookie recovery
What Happens: Create the cookie recovery service/command boundary and managed cookie/config write path.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for cookie fetch/import success, cancel, failure, and diagnostics.
Unlocks: Cookie recovery actions can be wired into Single and Batch flows.

## Planning Context

From phase-3-contract.md: the current sibling `tools/cookie_fetcher.py` opens Playwright and waits for terminal Enter, so validating must prove the app-triggered boundary before execution treats fetch-again as available.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- User-visible adapters need real production-boundary tests; a mocked cookie recovery success is not enough.

## File Scope

- src/services/cookieRecovery.ts or equivalent
- src-tauri/src/backend.rs or a narrow command module if native process invocation is needed
- sibling repo F:\Work\DouyinDownload\douyin-downloader\tools\cookie_fetcher.py only if validation says a non-terminal mode/wrapper is required
- focused cookie recovery tests

## Task

Implement the app-side cookie recovery boundary after validation defines it. Support success, cancel, missing runtime/dependency, timeout, and failure results. Write sanitized cookies to the app-used managed config or cookie file according to validation constraints.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove success/cancel/failure/missing-runtime result mapping.
- Tests prove cookie/config writes target the managed app config path, not an unrelated working directory.
- Raw process/browser errors are captured for Logs and not shown as primary main-panel text.
- No terminal Enter interaction is required for the production app path, or validation records fetch-again as blocked and the manual/import fallback remains available.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- App-triggered cookie capture is allowed by spawning tools.cookie_fetcher.py from a Tauri command with piped stdin; the app sends newline when the user confirms login.
- Use a separate short-lived cookie child process, not the long-running backend process state.
- Success requires exit code 0 plus validated app-used cookie/config state change containing msToken, ttwid, odin_tt, and passport_csrf_token.
- Do not rely on stdout, [INFO] Saved, [INFO] Updated config file, or exit code alone.
- Commit validated cookies into managed YAML; optional cookie JSON is only a cache/import artifact unless YAML explicitly enables auto-cookie loading.
- Missing Playwright, browser launch failure, invalid JSON, missing required keys, commit failure, and post-commit backend readiness failure are failures; user cancel must kill/wait the child and make no validated commit.
- Clarification: Do not edit the sibling downloader repo unless the Phase 3 validation constraints explicitly authorize a narrow wrapper/change. If that path is not available during execution, record fetch-again as blocked and keep manual/import fallback available.
close_reason: Completed: cookie recovery boundary with managed config commit and result mapping

title: Phase 3 / Story 2: Wire cookie recovery actions into Single and Batch
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: Cookie failures become actionable in Single and Batch workflows.
Unlocks Next: History can record meaningful recovered or unrecovered outcomes.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Cookie recovery
What Happens: Wire cookie guidance, fetch/manual/import actions, and retry guidance into Single job and Batch row failure surfaces.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for D11 user-facing recovery and retry after recovery.
Unlocks: Basic history can capture final outcomes after recovery/retry.

## Planning Context

From errorMapper.ts today: cookie-looking failures still say recovery is planned for a later phase. This bead replaces that dead-end with actions while preserving raw diagnostics separately.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- Keep fake proof explicitly scoped; UI tests can prove action wiring but cannot claim live cookie capture unless the boundary is exercised.

## File Scope

- src/services/errorMapper.ts
- src/app/App.tsx
- src/components/JobStatusPanel.tsx
- src/components/QueueTable.tsx
- src/components/BatchDownloadPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/tests/error-mapper.test.ts
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx

## Task

Update Single and Batch failure surfaces so cookie/auth errors show clear recovery actions and retry guidance. Integrate the cookie recovery service boundary from the prior bead and keep manual/import fallback visible when fetch-again is unavailable.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove cookie-looking Single failures show recovery action and retry guidance.
- Tests prove cookie-looking Batch failed rows show recovery action or row/bulk retry guidance without hiding row context.
- Tests prove cancel/failure from cookie recovery leaves the user in a clear state and preserves raw detail for Logs.
- Generic failures still show generic retry/check-logs guidance rather than cookie-specific actions.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- Single and Batch cookie recovery actions must call the validated cookie recovery boundary and then retry only after current-generation backend readiness is restored.
- Manual/import fallback must remain visible because Playwright/browser runtime is optional and Phase 4 packaging proof is still pending.
- Main UI shows friendly success/cancel/failure guidance only; raw stdout/stderr, missing-key lists, tracebacks, and config details go to Logs.
- Generic failures must stay generic and must not show cookie-specific actions.
close_reason: Completed: wired cookie recovery actions and retry guidance into single and batch flows

title: Phase 3 / Story 3: Build durable basic history store
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: The app can remember basic download outcomes across restarts.
Unlocks Next: The UI can show recent history after relaunch.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Basic history
What Happens: Create durable app-owned history storage for terminal Single and Batch outcomes.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for URL/time/status/output/result persistence.
Unlocks: History UI and integration with terminal job/row states.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: current server job storage is TTL/in-memory and cannot satisfy D12. History should be app-owned and basic.
From phase-3-contract.md: history must not become an advanced search/filter/result-history manager.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- Queue summaries should come from current row state; history after retry must record final current outcome, not accumulated stale attempts.

## File Scope

- src/services/historyStore.ts or equivalent
- src/tests/history-store.test.ts
- src/app/App.tsx only for narrow integration hooks if needed

## Task

Create a small persistent history store with a stable schema for URL, timestamp, mode, status, output/result location, and short error summary. Keep persistence app-owned and independent of backend in-memory jobs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove add/list/load-after-restart behavior.
- Tests prove malformed or missing history files fail safely without crashing app startup.
- Tests prove history caps or ordering keep the panel bounded.
- Store API supports recording final single outcomes and final batch row/queue outcomes without depending on backend job TTL.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- History must be app-owned and durable; never treat backend /api/v1/jobs as the persistent history store.
- Store writes should be idempotent upserts keyed by stable logical item ids: Single logical item id, and Batch batchRunId + rowId or equivalent per-row history id.
- Write visible history only from current terminal states. Backend job ids are attempt ids, not durable history ids.
- Retry should update the same visible history entry to the final current outcome; per-attempt failures belong in Logs.
- Corrupt/missing history files should fail friendly and route raw detail to Logs.
- Retention/order: keep at most 200 stored history entries, newest recordedAt/finishedAt first. On write/load, evict older entries beyond 200 after applying upserts. The UI may show fewer, but stored history should remain capped at 200 for Phase 3.
- Clarification: This bead should emit or expose structured diagnostic events for history load/write failures using the existing diagnostics seam. Bead .26 owns the final Logs surface rendering/routing for those events.
close_reason: Completed: durable app-owned history store with capped idempotent upsert and safe malformed-file handling

title: Phase 3 / Story 3: Render and populate basic history
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: Recent download outcomes are visible after app restart.
Unlocks Next: Logs/UAT can prove repeat-session behavior.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Basic history
What Happens: Record terminal Single and Batch outcomes and render a bounded basic history surface.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for persistent URL/time/status/output history.
Unlocks: Phase verification can cover relaunch persistence.

## Planning Context

From CONTEXT.md: D12 requires URL, time, status, and last known output folder/result location, while advanced search/filtering/history management is not required.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- Retry changes terminal outcomes; history should reflect current final row/job state after retry, not duplicate stale failure records as final truth.

## File Scope

- src/app/App.tsx
- src/components/HistoryPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/services/historyStore.ts
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx
- src/styles/app.css

## Task

Wire the history store into Single and Batch terminal states and add a bounded History surface. Keep it basic and scan-friendly: no search, filters, delete workflows, analytics, or full result browser.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove successful and failed Single outcomes are recorded with URL/time/status/output path.
- Tests prove Batch terminal outcomes record final row/queue state after retry.
- Tests prove history renders after simulated restart/load.
- UI remains basic and does not expose advanced history-management controls.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- History UI stays basic and bounded: no search, filters, delete flows, analytics, or full result browser.
- Single retry after recovery and Batch row retry must update the same visible history entry, not leave stale failed rows as final truth.
- Skipped batch rows, if shown, must be labeled as validation outcomes with source text/skip reason and no backend job id.
- Raw attempt diagnostics and retry details belong in Logs, not the basic History list.
close_reason: Completed: wired basic history store into single/batch terminal outcomes and added bounded history UI with restart persistence tests

title: Phase 3 / Story 4: Add separate Logs surface and event routing
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: Raw diagnostics move into a separate Logs surface for power users.
Unlocks Next: Phase 3 UAT can prove D13 and Phase 4 can package a complete app.

## Story Context

Story: Story 4 - Logs and proof
What Happens: Replace or promote the minimal diagnostics surface into a separate Logs tab/panel fed by backend, job, batch, settings, history, and cookie events.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for D13 logs separation and technical diagnostics.
Unlocks: Final Phase 3 verification bead.

## Planning Context

From phase-3-contract.md: main Single/Batch panels must remain friendly; technical details belong in Logs.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- Runtime evidence should include diagnostics capture; process existence and green tests are not enough for managed desktop behavior.

## File Scope

- src/services/logStore.ts or equivalent
- src/components/LogsPanel.tsx or update DiagnosticsPanel into a true Logs surface
- src/app/App.tsx
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx
- src/styles/app.css

## Task

Create a separate Logs tab/panel that captures backend lifecycle diagnostics, job polling errors, batch row failures, settings/config events, history persistence errors, and cookie recovery events. Keep logs hidden/separate by default from the main Single/Batch workflow.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests prove backend/job/batch/cookie diagnostics appear in Logs.
- Tests prove main workflow surfaces friendly messages rather than raw tracebacks or terminal streams.
- Tests prove Logs are separate from Single/Batch controls and can be opened by a power user.
- Log retention is bounded enough for Phase 3 and documented for Phase 4 packaging proof.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- Add an explicit capped app log store/panel or tab; do not expand Single or Batch panels with raw log text.
- Route structured events with at, level, source, message, and optional context such as job id, batch row id, cookie action id, or config version.
- Redact cookie values, authorization headers, config secrets, full cookie blobs, and cookie-like key/value pairs before persistence/display.
- Frontend log retention: keep newest 1000 app log events in memory/storage for Phase 3, evict oldest first. Logs panel should render newest-first or provide a clear chronological order without changing the cap.
- Rust/backend diagnostics retention: cap the Tauri backend diagnostics buffer at 500 events/lines, evict oldest first before the frontend pulls diagnostics.
- Tests must prove raw diagnostics appear in Logs while Single/Batch show friendly mapped messages only, and redaction happens before store/display.
close_reason: Completed: add separate Logs surface with structured routing, redaction, and retention caps

title: Phase 3: Verify recovery settings history and logs end to end
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 3 - Recovery, controls, history, and logs
What Changes: The phase has verification evidence for repeat-session settings/history, cookie recovery behavior, and Logs separation.
Unlocks Next: Phase 4 portable unzip-and-run packaging can begin after validation/execution/review are green.

## Story Context

Story: Story 4 - Logs and proof
What Happens: Run and document Phase 3 automated verification and UAT evidence.
Contributes To: The phase exit state as a whole.
Unlocks: Handoff to reviewing/next phase after validation and execution, not direct swarming.

## Planning Context

From phase-3-contract.md: UAT must prove settings/history survive relaunch and cookie/log behavior is either live-proven or explicitly blocked with validation evidence.

## Institutional Learnings

From history/learnings/20260508-desktop-queue-proof.md:
- Keep fake proof explicitly scoped; prove app-managed runtime paths separately from mocked UI or store tests.

## File Scope

- package scripts only if needed for verification
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-3-uat.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/evidence/* phase3 logs
- no broad feature implementation except small verification fixes from prior beads

## Task

Run and document Phase 3 verification after implementation beads complete. Include app tests, build, focused repeat-session proof for settings/history, cookie recovery proof or explicit blocker, and Logs separation evidence.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Phase 3 automated tests pass.
- npm run build passes.
- UAT evidence documents output/settings persistence across relaunch, cookie recovery/fallback behavior, history persistence, and Logs separation.
- Any live cookie or runtime proof that is unavailable is recorded as a blocker or scoped fake proof, not silently treated as green.
notes:
Validation constraints for execution:
- UAT evidence must disclose proof surfaces separately: fake tests, dev-runtime paths, live cookie capture if available, and blocked/unavailable paths.
- Verify settings/history survive relaunch, cookie recovery/fallback behavior follows the spike constraints, stale config blocks all submission paths, and Logs remain separate/redacted/bounded.
- Do not claim Phase 4 portable Playwright/browser packaging proof here; that remains Phase 4 unless explicitly performed.
- Clarification: Minimum validation commands are npm run test and npm run build after implementation, plus focused UAT evidence. Also prove backend health/readiness after a settings or cookie config-generation change, or record that runtime proof as blocked.

title: Phase 1 / Story 2: Add typed backend API client and config handoff
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The app can submit one URL to the managed backend using the same API contract it will later reuse for batch rows.
Unlocks Next: The Single mode form can call real client functions and enter active job state.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Single URL submission
What Happens: The app gets a typed client for health/download/job APIs plus a minimal output-folder/config handoff for the current app session.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that the selected output folder/config is honored when submitting one job.
Unlocks: The Single URL UI can submit a backend job and the status UI can poll by job id.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Use internal loopback REST to the app-managed backend and write a managed config file before backend start/job submission. Current server mode is single-job and in-memory, so do not assume app history from JobManager.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/services/backendClient.ts
- src/services/settingsStore.ts or Phase 1 equivalent
- src-tauri/src/settings.rs if native folder/config commands are needed
- src/tests/backend client/settings tests
- Backend repo files only if the current server cannot safely consume app-managed config/output path

## Task

Implement typed API client functions for health, create download job, get job, and list jobs. Add the minimal settings/config handoff needed for Phase 1: choose or keep an output folder and ensure the backend uses it for the submitted job. Keep persistence limited to what Phase 1 needs; full history is Phase 3.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover successful health, failed health, successful submit, failed submit, and get-job parsing.
- URL submission returns a typed job id/status object.
- Output folder/config handoff has tests proving the app-selected folder is used or serialized correctly.
- No test requires live Douyin network or real cookies.
- Any backend contract change is covered by Python server tests in the sibling backend repo.
notes: Spike constraints confirmed by .spikes/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/douyin-downloader-app-t1o/FINDINGS.md: config handoff is startup/session scoped, not a POST /api/v1/download field. Generate an app-owned runtime config outside backend config files, Tauri resources, PyInstaller extraction/resources, and bundled sidecar resources. Use absolute output paths only; default to an app-owned downloads folder under app data and create/validate it before backend start. Write config atomically using temp-write then rename. Current server deps capture FileManager(config.get(path)) at app startup, so changing output folder/config after readiness requires backend restart or submit remains blocked until readiness matches the current config version. Tests must prove generated config path and selected absolute output folder are serialized correctly, no bundled/backend config file is mutated, listJobs parses empty and active-job responses, and no test needs live Douyin/cookies.
close_reason: Completed: typed backend API client and runtime config handoff store with tests

title: Phase 1 / Story 2: Build Single mode URL submit flow
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The user can paste one in-scope Douyin URL, start a job, and see the UI enter active download state.
Unlocks Next: Story 3 can poll and render the active job status.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Single URL submission
What Happens: Single mode accepts one URL, handles blank/unsupported input gracefully, submits through the backend client, and records the active job id.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that Single mode can submit one URL through the backend job API.
Unlocks: Status/count UI can track the returned job id.

## Planning Context

From phase-1-contract.md: Phase 1 focuses only on one in-scope URL; full batch input/import/retry belongs to Phase 2.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/components/DownloadWorkspace.tsx
- src/components/ModeTabs.tsx
- src/components/SingleDownloadForm.tsx or equivalent
- src/services/queueStore.ts only if used for the single active job state
- src/tests/single submit UI tests

## Task

Build the Single mode UI flow for Phase 1. It should include URL input, start action, disabled/loading states while backend is not ready or submit is in progress, and friendly submit errors. Keep Batch visible as a first-class tab/toggle per D9, but mark its execution as not implemented until Phase 2 without pretending batch works.

## Acceptance Criteria

- UI tests cover blank URL, disabled backend-not-ready state, submit success, and submit failure.
- Successful submit stores the active job id and moves the workspace into active-job state.
- Batch tab/toggle is visible with equal weight but does not expose fake Phase 2 behavior.
- Main screen remains clean and status-forward; no terminal log stream appears in the main download surface.
notes: Submit constraints from validation spikes: block submit until backend readiness corresponds to the current settings/config version. If the output folder changed after backend start, restart backend cleanly before POST /api/v1/download or keep submit disabled with an actionable UI state. Batch remains visible with equal weight but non-executing for Phase 1. URL handling contract: lightweight frontend validation accepts non-empty http/https URLs whose hostname is exactly douyin.com, ends with .douyin.com, is exactly iesdouyin.com, or ends with .iesdouyin.com, including short links. Reject lookalikes such as notdouyin.com, douyin.com.evil.test, evil-douyin.com, and non-http schemes. Blank input shows inline required message. Unsupported URL shows unsupported message and does not call POST. Backend unsupported RuntimeError remains handled by Story 3 fallback because backend is final authority. UI tests must cover blank URL, unsupported non-Douyin URL, lookalike host rejection, accepted Douyin URL submit, backend-not-ready disabled state, config-version mismatch disabled/restart state, submit success, and submit failure.
close_reason: Completed: single mode submit flow with URL validation, submit gating, and UI coverage

title: Phase 1 / Story 3: Render job polling status counts and friendly errors
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The user can see what happened to a submitted single job without reading terminal output.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 can reuse the same job status model for queue rows.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Single job status and result actions
What Happens: The app polls backend job state, renders pending/running/success/failed with total/success/failed/skipped counts, and maps raw backend errors to practical friendly messages.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that the single-download loop is observable and actionable.
Unlocks: Open-folder/result actions and Phase 1 UAT proof.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Main UI should show friendly status/errors while raw diagnostics stay in a separate diagnostics surface. Current backend errors may be raw strings like RuntimeError, so UI needs a mapping layer.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/services/jobPolling.ts
- src/services/errorMapper.ts
- src/components/JobStatusPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/tests/job polling/status/error tests

## Task

Implement job polling and status rendering for one active job. Stop polling on terminal states. Show job status, active URL/job id, and success/failed/skipped counts. Add a small error mapper so common backend failures become user-facing text while preserving raw detail for diagnostics.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover pending, running, success, failed, 404/unknown job, and polling error states.
- Polling stops on terminal states and cleans up timers/tasks.
- Counts render correctly from backend job fields.
- Friendly error text appears in the main UI; raw details are not dumped as the primary message.
- No tests require live Douyin network calls.
notes: Error mapping contract: map backend unreachable/network timeout to 'Downloader backend is not ready. Restart the app backend and try again.' with raw detail in diagnostics; 404/unknown job to 'This download job is no longer available. Start the download again.'; RuntimeError containing Unsupported URL to 'This link is not supported by the current downloader.'; cookie/auth terms such as cookie, msToken, ttwid, login, 401, 403 to 'Douyin login cookies may be missing or expired. Cookie recovery is planned for a later phase; update cookies manually for now.'; other failed job errors to 'Download failed. Check diagnostics for technical details.' Fallback must preserve raw error in diagnostics and never dump raw tracebacks as the primary main-panel message. Tests must cover each mapping plus fallback.
close_reason: Completed: added job polling terminal handling, status counts UI, and friendly error mapping with tests

title: Phase 1 / Story 3: Add open output folder action and diagnostics surface
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: After a terminal job state, the user can take a practical next action: open the output folder used by the job.
Unlocks Next: Later history and batch result actions can reuse the same native open-folder and diagnostics patterns.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Single job status and result actions
What Happens: Terminal job UI exposes an open-folder action and diagnostics remain separate from the main download surface.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that success/failure states have practical actions and logs are not streamed into the main UI.
Unlocks: Phase 2/3 result and logs surfaces.

## Planning Context

From phase-1-contract.md: Phase 1 result action may open the selected output folder; full result-history viewer and advanced logs are later phases.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src-tauri/src/settings.rs or native command module for opening folders
- src/components/ResultActions.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/LogsPanel.tsx only as minimal diagnostics surface if needed
- src/services/diagnosticsStore.ts if needed
- src/tests/result action/diagnostics tests

## Task

Add the native command and UI action to open the current output folder after a terminal job. Add the minimum diagnostics surface needed to prove raw backend messages are captured separately from the main workflow. Do not build full Phase 3 history/log management.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests or command-level checks prove open-folder is called with the selected output folder.
- The action is visible after success and available after failure when an output folder exists.
- Diagnostics/raw backend text is accessible separately and not streamed into the main download panel.
- Folder path handling is Windows-safe and handles missing/unset folder gracefully.
notes: Executor clarification: if no output folder is configured, disable the open-folder action and show a short friendly message telling the user to choose an output folder first. If the selected absolute folder no longer exists, keep raw filesystem detail in diagnostics and show a friendly main-panel error that the folder cannot be opened until it is recreated or changed. Open-folder action must use the selected absolute output folder recorded by app settings/config handoff, not infer from backend job metadata because current job responses do not include a result location. Do not shell to arbitrary paths. Diagnostics/raw backend output must remain separate from the main download panel and be available through a minimal diagnostics surface only. The action is visible after success and available after failure when an output folder exists.
close_reason: Completed: open output folder action with separate diagnostics surface

title: Phase 1: Verify one desktop-managed download loop end to end
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The team has proof that one desktop-managed single-download loop works or a precise blocker explaining why it cannot yet work.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 batch planning/execution can proceed only after Phase 1 validation and execution are genuinely green.

## Story Context

Story: Phase 1 closure - prove the integrated loop
What Happens: Run the Phase 1 verification suite and a manual/dev UAT path for app launch, backend readiness, single submit, status polling, terminal state, and open-folder action.
Contributes To: The phase exit state as a whole.
Unlocks: Handoff to reviewing/next phase after execution, not before validation.

## Planning Context

From phase-1-contract.md: Demo checklist must prove desktop launch, backend health readiness, output folder, single URL submit, status/count polling, and success/failure action. Real Douyin credentials may be unavailable, so a documented fake/test backend path is acceptable for automated proof, but runtime integration must still be exercised.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- package/test/build scripts as needed
- docs or history update only if needed for UAT notes
- no broad feature implementation files unless fixing verification gaps from prior beads

## Task

Run and document the Phase 1 verification path after implementation beads complete. This includes app tests, backend tests for any changed server contract, app build, Tauri dev/build smoke if available, and a manual/dev UAT of the single-download loop with either the real backend or an explicit fake backend when live Douyin/cookies are unavailable.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All Phase 1 automated tests pass.
- Backend tests pass if backend files changed.
- App build passes.
- UAT evidence covers desktop launch, backend readiness, one URL submit, status/count rendering, terminal state, and open-folder action.
- Any unavailable proof is reported as a blocker, not silently treated as green.
notes: Rescue verification completed on 2026-05-08. npm test (backend-lifecycle/settings-store/app-shell) passed 22/22; npm run build passed; tauri smoke evidence at history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/evidence/phase1-tauri-smoke-ampere.json shows health_ok=true with /api/v1/health status ok and clean teardown (no lingering managed python/app processes).

title: Phase 2 / Story 1: Build batch queue parser and row model
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 2 - Batch queue is first-class
What Changes: Batch mode can turn pasted/imported text into stable queue rows before any backend work starts.
Unlocks Next: Queue execution can submit only valid waiting rows.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - Batch input and import
What Happens: Create the app-side batch row model, parser, validation states, and import boundary.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for visible queue rows, invalid/skipped rows, and deterministic input behavior.
Unlocks: Batch panel UI can render queue rows without embedding parsing rules in React.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Batch queue should be app-owned and mapped to backend jobs; current server has no batch endpoint, pause, cancel, or persistent jobs.
From phase-2-contract.md: invalid/unsupported/duplicate rows must not start backend jobs.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/services/batchQueue.ts or equivalent new service module
- src/tests/batch-queue.test.ts or equivalent focused tests
- no backend files

## Task

Implement the batch queue parser/model as public behavior. It should accept multiline text and imported text content, normalize rows, assign stable row ids, identify supported Douyin/iesdouyin URLs, mark blank/unsupported/duplicate inputs as skipped or invalid, and expose aggregate helpers needed by the UI.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover pasted multiline input, imported text content, blank lines, unsupported hosts, lookalike hosts, duplicates, and valid Douyin/iesdouyin URLs.
- Valid waiting rows are distinguishable from skipped/invalid rows.
- No backend client calls happen in this bead.
- The model vocabulary can support waiting, running, success, failed, skipped, and retry eligibility in later beads.
notes:
Validation spike constraints:
- Define app-owned row state as authoritative for valid/waiting/running/success/failed/skipped, retry eligibility, current job/attempt, and aggregate helpers.
- Parser rows for blank, unsupported, duplicate, or invalid inputs must not call the backend.
- Expose display-ready counts so UI beads can render one compact totals strip without inventing ad hoc row fields.
- Use URL parsing for host validation: accept only exact douyin.com/iesdouyin.com hostnames or their subdomains, and reject suffix/lookalike hosts such as douyin.com.evil.test.
- Duplicate detection should be based on the normalized supported URL value used for submission; duplicates stay visible as skipped/non-ready rows and are not retry eligible unless the input is edited/reparsed into a unique supported URL.
close_reason: Completed: added batch queue parser/model with URL validation, duplicate handling, and totals helpers

title: Phase 2 / Story 1: Replace Batch placeholder with input/import UI
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 2 - Batch queue is first-class
What Changes: Batch mode is no longer a placeholder; users can create and inspect a queue.
Unlocks Next: Queue execution can be wired to visible row state.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - Batch input and import
What Happens: Replace the Batch placeholder with paste/import controls and a queue table shell.
Contributes To: Phase exit states for visible batch rows and skipped/invalid feedback.
Unlocks: Story 2 can add Start behavior to the existing Batch surface.

## Planning Context

From phase-2-contract.md: Single and Batch are equal-weight tabs, and Batch must support pasted multiline URLs plus imported text-file URLs.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/app/App.tsx
- src/components/BatchDownloadPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/QueueTable.tsx or equivalent
- src/tests/app-shell.test.tsx and/or new Batch UI tests
- src/styles/app.css

## Task

Build the Batch UI around the parser/model from bead .8. Users should be able to paste URLs, trigger import through a testable adapter/boundary, see rows with clear status, and understand skipped/invalid rows before starting execution.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Batch tab renders real controls instead of placeholder-only content.
- Tests prove queue rows appear from pasted text and imported content.
- Invalid/skipped rows are visible and not treated as ready-to-run.
- UI remains a restrained Windows utility, not a dense developer dashboard.
- No live backend or filesystem dialog is required for automated tests.
notes:
Validation spike constraints:
- Keep Batch inside the existing full-width mode surface as one bounded workflow.
- Render paste/import input, compact toolbar shell, one totals region, and queue rows; do not add nested dashboard cards.
- Invalid/skipped rows must be visible before execution and must not appear ready to run.
close_reason: Completed: replace Batch placeholder with paste/import queue UI and tests

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Can Tauri manage backend sidecar lifecycle for dev and packaged-compatible runtime?
close_reason: YES: Tauri can manage backend lifecycle for Phase 1 using dev Python now and sidecar-compatible abstraction later; readiness must poll /api/v1/health and packaged mode needs scoped sidecar capabilities.

title: Spike: Phase 3 - logs separation and retention
close_reason: YES: Phase 3 can capture backend/job/batch/cookie diagnostics in a separate bounded Logs surface if execution adds explicit retention caps, redaction, structured log routing, and keeps raw details out of Single/Batch.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Can backend packaging boundary support the Phase 1 architecture?
close_reason: YES: Backend packaging boundary supports the architecture if packaging uses a server-only sidecar entrypoint; naive run.py PyInstaller path is not production-ready due cli import/path and Rich banner encoding issues.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Can output folder and config handoff be done safely for one app session?
close_reason: YES: Output folder/config handoff is safe for one app-managed backend session when app writes absolute-path runtime config before backend start and restarts backend after config changes.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - backend config handoff yes/no
close_reason: YES: Config handoff is safe only as startup/session-scoped app-owned runtime config with absolute output path; folder changes require backend restart or blocked submit.

title: Spike: Phase 3 - app-triggered cookie capture boundary
close_reason: YES: app can trigger cookie capture by controlling the cookie_fetcher child stdin from Tauri; success must require exit 0 plus validated app-used cookie/config state change, while cancel/failure are child-process and validation-state outcomes.

title: Spike: Phase 3 - settings backend readiness contract
close_reason: YES: settings changes must be generation-gated, block single/batch submissions, restart the managed backend, and mark ready only after health passes for the current config.
